One is, um, knowing
what the actual quantum of the costs were.
And the other thing is knowing that this is a business cost
that you could reasonably and foreseeably expect.
So the Airport's Cost Recovery Act has been on the box
since 2014.
So it is well known that this is a liability
that a new restarting airport could be liable for.
It's the first point. Um, the second point is that, um,
there's been informal discussions
with Dunedin Airport for some time.
Um, and even before we started consulting on this, um,
Dunedin airport did know that it was possible
that they could be liable for these costs.
Then we went through a process of formal consultation
through our discussion document.
And, um, I'm not quite sure if the exact timeline, Daniel,
as to when the paperwork was signed,
but our view is this is a foreseeable business cost.
Fair enough. They didn't know the exact quantum
that, that that is true.
And that's partly because we needed
to get those regs in place to enable, to know
what was the parameters of the regulations,
which then drives the costs.
Thank you. Now, ministers have been really clear with us
that they expected us to work in good
faith, and we've done that.
Thank you. Um, and just one final question
and then we'll, um, thank you very much for your time.
The, um, just what you've said there,
that is I think a key point.
Mm-hmm. When was the last time that the Airports Recovery,
uh, airport Cost Recovery Act was used
to charge a lumps lump sum?
So this, this is the first time,
'cause we needed the regulations in place in order
to fully implement the act.
And when were the regulations in place?
Um, since, uh, 10 March, 2025.
Okay. Thank you very much.
This is an important part of, um,
accountability of executive government.
For the people who follow along on this closely,
there won't be many, but thank you very much for coming,
outing our questions.
